coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photodetection in organic semiconductor single crystal sensitized by a tailored synergistic absorption of capsule-shaped chlorophyll derived CQDs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Yangyang
Wei, Jinkai
Hu, Yongxu
Ren, Jianzhou
Guo, Hongling
Lv, Hao
Geng, Chong
Wang, Zhongwu
Xu, Shu
Wang, Fang
Zheng, Lei
Zhang, Kailiang
Li, Liqiang
coredata.dc:description: Organic single-crystal phototransistors have emerged as one of the most promising light signal detectors for their minimal defects, high mobility and light sensitivity. However, fully exploiting their advantages in advanced flexible optoelectronic applications remains great difficulty and challenging due to the lack of efficient methods for upgrading the photoresponse of delicate organic crystalline materials. Herein, we developed a capsule-shaped chlorophyll derived carbon quantum dots (Chl-CQDs) to enhance the single-crystal photodetection. Through a multi-site carbonization of chlorophyll peripheral substituents, the CQDs were produced around the chlorophyll tetrapyrrole macrocyclicvia. It makes Chl-CQDs combine the light absorption advantages of chlorophyll and CQDs, and exhibit an amazing synergistic absorption behavior. Compared to pristine devices of 2,6-diphenyl anthracene (DPA), the key figures of merit such as P, R, and D* of the Chl-CQDs/DPA heterophototransistors are improved by over 6000 times, 5 times, and 200 times, respectively. Additionally, an ultra fast charge transfer dynamics (τ rise = 35 ms and τ decay = 45 ms) is observed at such unique type II heterointerface. These results not only offer a new approach for improving the photoresponse of single crystal organic phototransistors, but also make it possible to be superior to that of silicon-based devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic single crystal
Carbon quantum dots
Synergistic absorption
Enhanced photoresponse
Hybrid phototransistor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials synthesis and growth of capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs and DPA single crystals.
Device fabrication and characterization of Chl-CQDs/DPA heterotransistors.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City (22JCQNJC00910), Science and Technology Planning Project of Tianjin City (20ZYQCGX00070), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0405600, 2018YFA0703200), Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City (18JCYBJC85700, 18JCZDJC30500), National Natural Science Foundation of China (62001326, 61274113, 61404091, 62304152) in part by the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Functional Materials for Information (SKL202007).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Assembly and characterization of Chl-CQDs/DPA hybrid structure. (a) The photograph of dispersed Chl-CQDs in ethanol solution, synthesized by sycamore leaves with a simple and environment friendly hydrothermal process. (b) HRTEM images of capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs and their internal CQDs of as-used Chl-CQDs solution and spin-coated film. (c) The EDS element mapping (C, N, O, Mg) of Chl-CQDs, suggesting the uniform distribution of Chl-CQDs. (d) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of Chl-CQDs sensitized DPA single crystal via spin-coating processes. (e) Typical optical image of DPA crystals on a SiO2/Si substrate. (f) The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the DPA crystal. G, h) AFM images of DPA single crystal before and after sensitization.

Spectroscopic characterizations of DPA crystal, Chl-CQDs complex and Chl-CQDs/DPA hybrid structure. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the ethanol-solvothermal leaf, ethanol-leaf, DMF-solvothermal leaf and DMF-leaf solution, respectively. (b) FTIR spectrum of Ethanol-solvothermal leaf (Chl-CQDs) and Ethanol-leaf. (c) Room temperature photoluminescence (PL) measurements of DPA crystal, Chl-CQDs film and the DPA crystal/Chl-CQDs heterostructure under an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. (d) Typical UV–vis absorption spectra of DPA crystalline film and Chl-CQDs/DPA hybrid structure.

Device performance of Chl-CQDs/DPA heterotransistors. (a) The schematic device structure and optical image of heterotransistor. (b) The estimated Type II energy band alignment between DPA and Chl-CQDs before and after contact based on the estimated valence band offset and work function difference in UPS. After contact, charge-transfer dipoles could be formed at the Chl-CQDs/DPA interface. (c) Multiple transfer curves of heterotransistor under a bias of −40 V. The ratios of device channel length/width are about 2/3. (d) Mobility distributions of Chl-CQDs/DPA-based heterotransistors and their pristine DPA OFETs based on 20 devices, respectively.

High-performance hybrid phototransistors based on capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs and DPA single crystals. (a) Schematic illustration of the hybrid photodetector under light excitation. (b, c) Typical transfer curves of hybrid photodetector and pristine DPA device at different light power densities conditions. (d, e) Typical output curves of hybrid devices and pristine device under the white light illumination of 450 μW cm−2. (f-h) The values of P, R and D* for the hybrid phototransistor under optical irradiation with a series of incident intensities. (i) Typical transient current in hybrid phototransistors when applying pulsed light illumination (white light, 27.6 μW cm−2).
The values of P, R and D* before and after sensitization for the DPA single-crystal OPTs tested at 450 μW cm−2.

Working mechanism of Chl-CQDs sensitized DPA single-crystal OPTs. (a) The possible synergistic absorption mode of Chlorophll and CQDs. (b) The proposed interfacial energy band diagrams illustrating the different charge separation efficiency at a type II Chl-CQDs/DPA heterostructure under different field-effect modulation.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photodetection in organic semiconductor single crystal sensitized by a tailored synergistic absorption of capsule-shaped chlorophyll derived CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Tianjin Key Laboratory of Molecular Optoelectronic Sciences

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Electronic Materials and Devices
School of Electronics and Information Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Electronic Materials and Devices, School of Electronics and Information Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300401, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on the tailored synergistic absorption by a capsule-shaped Chl-CDs composite’s abundant light absorption sites and multiple π-electron systems, we have successfully achieved a significant enhancement of the photoresponse of DPA single-crystal OPTs. The hybrid phototransistor of Chl-CQDs sensitized DPA shows a fast photoresponse speed and high photogain up to 2.8×105 at the unique type II interface. Moreover, compared to the pristine DPA device, the P, R and D* of heterostructure are raised over 6000 times, 5 times and 200 times, respectively. These results may open up a new avenue for optimizing the photoresponse of OPTs towards advanced flexible optoelectronic applications.

Organic single-crystal phototransistors have emerged as one of the most promising light signal detectors for their minimal defects, high mobility and light sensitivity. However, fully exploiting their advantages in advanced flexible optoelectronic applications remains great difficulty and challenging due to the lack of efficient methods for upgrading the photoresponse of delicate organic crystalline materials. Herein, we developed a capsule-shaped chlorophyll derived carbon quantum dots (Chl-CQDs) to enhance the single-crystal photodetection. Through a multi-site carbonization of chlorophyll peripheral substituents, the CQDs were produced around the chlorophyll tetrapyrrole macrocyclicvia. It makes Chl-CQDs combine the light absorption advantages of chlorophyll and CQDs, and exhibit an amazing synergistic absorption behavior. Compared to pristine devices of 2,6-diphenyl anthracene (DPA), the key figures of merit such as P, R, and D* of the Chl-CQDs/DPA heterophototransistors are improved by over 6000 times, 5 times, and 200 times, respectively. Additionally, an ultra fast charge transfer dynamics (τ rise = 35 ms and τ decay = 45 ms) is observed at such unique type II heterointerface. These results not only offer a new approach for improving the photoresponse of single crystal organic phototransistors, but also make it possible to be superior to that of silicon-based devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the several decades of development, organic semiconductors (OSCs) materials have demonstrated significant potential in various functional optoelectronic applications such as organic field-effect transistors (OFETs), organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), and organic phototransistors (OPTs) [1–3]. These devices serve as the foundation for next-generation flexible electronics. OPTs, in particular, have emerged as highly promising light signal detectors due to their tailorable optoelectronic properties, low-temperature fabrication, efficient synergetic modulation by bias and light, and high integration ability [4–6]. However, the low mobility of most organic photo-responsive materials hinders the efficient transportation and collection of charge carriers, which limits the further advancement of OPTs [7]. Fortunately, in recent years, organic single-crystal semiconductors have emerged as a standout option among numerous photoresponsive materials and showed advantages such as long exciton diffusion length, minimal defects, high mobility and light sensitivity [8]. This makes the single-crystal OPTs comparable and even superior to that of silicon based phototransistors. Therefore, the organic single crystals have garnered significant attention and demonstrated great potential in next-generation optoelectronic circuits. However, due to the lack of further enhancing the photoresponse of delicate organic single crystals, it is still a formidable challenge to effectively harness the advantages of organic single-crystal semiconductors for the advanced optoelectronic applications.

A series of optimized strategies have been used to enhance the photoresponse of phototransistors via functioned heterojunctions, such as facilitating formation of a built-in electric field, improving separation of electron-hole pairs, and fast generating photoelectric current[9–13]. Among the demonstrated heterojunctions, those consisting of colloidal quantum dots (QDs) coupled with semiconductor materials have shown great potential in engineering response spectra in detectors and constructing high-sensitivity devices. This is due to their high extinction coefficient and size-tunable bandgap[10,14–17]. For instance, high sensitivity (>107 A/W) has been achieved through the photogating effect based on charge separation at the hetero-interface between QDs and graphene[15]. However, the preparation of these devices are often complex and require high-temperature conditions and multi-component solvents, which can be destructive to organic photoresponsive materials, particularly organic single crystals. Consequently, there are few reports on QDs sensitized single-crystal OPTs with both high mobility and superior light sensitivity. Fortunately, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) scarcely damage the organic crystalline structure for their simple-component solvents and low-temperature compatible device preparation process. Moreover, the CQDs exhibit abundant source of precursors, minimal toxic chemicals and friendly solvents when leaves serve as the carbon sources[18]. Furthermore, the extensive π-electron systems exist in chlorophyll’s N-based tetrapyrrole macrocyclic rings and serve as key sites for light absorption and emission of leaf. Therefore, the leaf-derived CQDs demonstrate great potential in engineering response spectra in detectors. However, the study of such CQDs with the afore-mentioned advantages on the photoresponse behavior of single-crystal OPTs remains unconfirmed.

Herein, we developed a simple and environmentally friendly sensitization process to significantly enhance the photoresponse of 2,6-diphenyl anthracene (DPA) single-crystal OPTs via using a capsule-shaped chlorophyll-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Chl-CQDs). With the unique Chl-CQDs layer coated onto DPA crystal, the Chl-CQD/DPA hetero-structure combine abundant light absorption sites and multiple π-electron systems, which induced by a synergistic tailoring of absorption behavior through the closely distribution of CQDs in the chlorophyll matrix. Compared to the pristine bottom-gate-top-contact DPA device, the P, R, and D* values of the Chl-CQD/DPA hetero-phototransistor are improved by over 6000 times, 5 times, and 200 times, respectively. Besides, the hybrid phototransistor demonstrates a high photogain greater than 2.8 × 105 and very fast charge transfer dynamics at the heterointerface (τrise = 35 ms, τdecay = 45 ms, white light power = 27.6 μw/cm2), which can be attributed to the efficient sensitized structure of capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs and the established type II interface with efficient charge separation. These impressive results not only suggest the great potential of Chl-CQDs/DPA-based phototransistor in optoelectronic applications, but also pave the way for fully exploiting their advantages of single-crystal OPTs in next-generation advanced optoelectronics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials synthesis and growth of capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs and DPA single crystals.
ce:section.ce:para: The capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs are synthesized by solvothermal reaction of sycamore leaves, which were picked in Tianjin University of Technology (Tianjin, China). In details, fresh sycamore leaves were firstly cleaned, dried and ground into powder by a grinder. Then 1.0 g powder was scattered in 20 mL anhydrous ethanol purchased from Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). After extraction, the chlorophyll-based ethanol solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) lined autoclave (50 mL) after solvent extraction. They was performed in an oven at 140 ℃ for 4 hours. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through molecular sieve to purified the products. The solvent removal via rotary evaporation and drying under vacuum. Finally, the purified product is dissolved in anhydrous ethanol to form a capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs solution. For comparison, the extraction of the chlorophyll was extracted by the same method to the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs except for the solvothermal reaction process. The pore size of the molecular sieve is 0.2 µm and the Chl-CQDs solution is further purified with molecular sieve after the centrifuge purification. A two-zone horizontal tube furnace was employed for PVT, and the system was evacuated with a mechanical pump, which kept at 17 Pa. A quartz boat with DPA powder of the raw materials was placed in the high-temperature zone (165 °C) for 2 hours. High-purity argon was used as the carries gas (20 sccm). Then, DPA single crystals of the raw materials were further obtained on the OTS modified SiO2 (300 nm)/Si substrates in the low temperature zone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Device fabrication and characterization of Chl-CQDs/DPA heterotransistors.
ce:section.ce:para: SiO2/Si wafers containing 300 nm-thick SiO2 layers were successively cleaned with deionized water, isopropanol, deionized water, piranha solution (70/30 vol./vol. H2SO4/H2O2), deionized water, and isopropanol. Surface modification of Si/SiO2 wafer with OTS was then carried out by vapor deposition method at a vacuum chamber (0.1 Pa). The Si wafer was first dried at 90 °C (1.5 hours) to remove the moisture and then kept at 120 °C (2 hours) to allow for the deposition of OTS. After cooling down to room temperature, the substrate was washed successively with n-hexane, trichloromethane and isopropanol for following device fabrications. DPA crystals need to be pre-transferred onto the OTS modified SiO2 (300 nm)/Si and then 35 nm Au electrodes were deposited in vacuum through a manually aligned shadow mask with a substrate temperature of 23 °C and a vacuum of 1 × 10-6 torr. At last, the Chl-CQDs in ethanol solution is spin-coated on the DPA single-crystal devices at a speed of 2000 rpm. for 10 s, and the sample was then annealed at 65 °C for 20 min to improve neck connection and therefore carrier mobility within Chl-CQDs. All the measurements of OFETs were tested on Agilent B1500 semiconductor parameter analyzer with micromanipulator 6150 probe station at room temperature in glove box.

UV–vis spectra were obtained on SHIMADZU UV-3600 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a HITACHI F-7000 spectrofluorometer. Optical microscopy images were taken with a Nikon ECLIPSE Ci-POL polarized optical microscope with a blue filter. SAED patterns of DPA single crystal were measured by a Themis 300 cryo-transmission electron microscope. UPS (KRATOS Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer) data were obtained using He I (h = 21.22 eV) as the excitation source with a base pressure > 2 × 10-9 torr. Intelligent mode AFM was performed by using a Bruker Dimension Icon. Energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) characterization was performed on an EDAX Octane Plus Det operating at 1.76 keV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurements were carried out on a Thermo-Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer.

The capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs composite was designed and synthesized by solvothermal reaction of sycamore leaves in friendly ethanol solution after solvent extraction, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The morphology of the as-prepared capsule structure Chl-CQDs was performed by HRTEM, as shown in Fig. 1b, which exhibits the nanoparticles packaged in the capsule matrix. The size of single capsule structure is about 50 nm. Moreover, the almost all these quasi-spherical nanoparticles have the well-resolved lattice fringes with the distance about 0.2 nm, which corresponds the graphene (100) facet and evidences the formation of crystalline carbon dots structure (CQDs)[19]. Compared to the larger size of the single capsule structure, the diameter of the CQDs is about 2–5 nm, which allows a certain number of CQDs to be closely distributed in the capsule matrix. This can be evidenced in the similar Chl-CQDs structures as-prepared via different temperatures of the solvothermal reaction, as shown in Fig. S1(a). With the rise of reaction temperature, the PL peak of Chl-CQDs at about 673 nm has a slight blue shift from 674 to 671 nm, and the PL peak around 495 nm has a certain increment shown in Fig. S1(b), which is due to the increased amount of CQDs. Meanwhile, a new lattice plane space of CQDs with about 0.32 nm is observed in the high-resolution TEM images of Chl-CQDs from both 140 and 170 ℃ reaction temperature. And this distance (0.32 nm) corresponds to the spaces between the two 0.2 nm lattice fringes of graphene (100) facet structures stacked on top of each other, and these two kinds of lattice fringes form the major part of the CQDs[20]. Furthermore, the Raman spectra of Chl-CQDs through different reaction temperature were recorded after drop-casting the Chl-CQDs solutions on Si substrate, as shown in Fig. S1(c). The peak at 1330 cm−1 associates with the disorder or defect related peak (D peak) and another peak at 1577 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane vibrational mode of sp2 graphitic carbon (G peak). These broad D and G peaks indicate the presence of CQDs in Chl-CQDs structure and much defect states or surface functional groups between the interface of CQDs and chlorophyll[21]. Therefore, the high-density distribution of defect states or surface functional groups could enhance the energy transfer (e.g., Förster resonance energy transfer) among the CQDs and chlorophyll, which increases the absorption of the Chl-CQDs[22]. Furthermore, the EDS element mapping (C, N, O, Mg) of Chl-CQDs is exhibited to evidence the existence of chlorophyll in the capsule matrix, as shown in Fig. 1c. The distribution of the Mg element is like the N element and exhibits the vacant sites with the similar size to the CQDs. It indicates that the chlorophyll is distributed around the CQDs and still retains N-based light absorption sites in the capsule matrix. In contrast, no crystalline carbon structure or clearly capsule-shaped assembly were found in the products of the room temperature extraction by ethanol or dimethyl formamide (DMF), as shown in Fig. S2 and named Ethanol-leaf and DMF-leaf, respectively. Meanwhile, the dispersed CQDs without the capsule-shaped assembly structure were observed in the DMF solvothermal process based products (DMF-solvothermal leaf). Therefore, the ethanol solvothermal process makes the combination of the chlorophyll and CQDs and assembles the capsule-shaped structure (Chl-CQDs).

2,6-diphenylanthracene (DPA) single crystal was selected as the organic photoresponsive layer due to its photoelectric figures of merit[23]. Here, high-quality DPA single crystals were prepared using the physical vapor transport (PVT) method under a high-purity nitrogen gas flow with a pressure of 17 pa. The DPA crystal powder was sublimated at a temperature of 165 °C, and the growth process was maintained for 2 hours. Then, Bottom-gate-top-contact DPA single-crystal OFETs were fabricated on an OTS modified SiO2 (300 nm)/Si substrate using pre-transferred DPA crystals and 35-nm Au electrodes deposited in a vacuum. Finally, using a simple spin-coating process illustrated in Fig. 1d, the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs composite dissolved in friendly ethanol solution was directly assembled on the pre-prepared.

DPA devices. The sample was then annealed at 65 °C for 20 min to improve the neck connection and thus enhance carrier mobility within the Chl-CQDs. An optical image of DPA crystals on a SiO2/Si substrate is shown in Fig. 1e and Fig. 1f, which displays the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the DPA crystal, indicating the high quality of the prepared crystals. The surface morphologies and thicknesses of the DPA crystals and crystal/Chl-CQDs heterostructure were examined by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The optimized thickness of the Chl-CQDs layer after the spin-coating process was found to be approximately 9 nm (Fig. 1g, h).

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the leaf derivatives solutions synthesized by room temperature extraction or solvothermal process have been staggered for better visualization, as shown in Fig. 2 a. As the reference, the chlorophyll extracted by ethanol (Ethanol-leaf) exhibits the main peaks at about 225, 413 and 664 nm, which represent the original absorption characteristic of the sycamore leaves chlorophyll. Moreover, the original absorption at about 225 to 271 nm was disappeared when the DMF served as the reaction solvent. And the DMF enhanced the absorption at about 271 to 363 nm. Compared to the room temperature extraction (DMF-leaf), the DMF solvothermal process (DMF-solvothermal leaf) decreased all the main peaks of the chlorophyll original absorption at about 225, 413 and 664 nm. Different with DMF, the ethanol solvothermal process (ethanol-solvothermal leaf), which produced the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs, enhanced all the main peaks of the chlorophyll original absorption and filled the absorption trough of 225 to 363 nm. It indicates that the ethanol solvothermal reaction products could achieve an enhancement over the whole chlorophyll original absorption spectrum. According to reports, the absorption peak around 250–270 nm is due to π-π* transitions of C = C group in graphene and CQDs[21,24]. Therefore, the absorbance peak of the ethanol-solvothermal leaf at 251 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the C = C bond. As a magnesium porphyrin derivative, the chlorophyll contains an extensive π-electron system and enables the electronic transitions between π to π* orbitals, which makes the large absorption bands around 600–800 nm and 380–430 nm[25]. Correspondingly, the absorbance peaks of the ethanol-solvothermal leaf around 429 nm and 664 nm are like the sycamore leaf chlorophyll extracted by ethanol, which indicates the existence of the chlorophyll. Therefore, the ethanol solvothermal process enables the combination of CQDs and chlorophyll in capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs, which makes the synergistic effect induced absorption enhancement. Furthermore, the structural properties of the chlorophyll and Chl-CQDs were compared by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b). The main peak positions of the Chl-CQDs are same to the chlorophyll. The IR bands at about 3200–3600 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H, which come from ethanol induced solvothermal method. The peak at about 2920 cm−1 is attributed to the C-H stretching vibrations of aliphatic groups. Stretching vibrations of C = O groups from chlorophyll molecules with conjugation, and C = C are located at about 1750 and 1620 cm−1, respectively. The existence of stretching vibration bands at about 1500–1360 cm−1 demonstrates the existence of C-C and C-N groups in the pyrrole ring of chlorophyll. The peak at about 1040 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of C-O from the chlorophyll or the alcoholic C-OH[20]. The similar peak positions between the chlorophyll and Chl-CQDs indicates that the chlorophyll is one of the light absorptions center inner the Chl-CQDs structure. Moreover, compared to the chlorophyll, the new C = C group band appeared at about 1620 cm−1 implies the formation of CQDs. This is further supported by the high-resolution scan of the C1s region according to XPS survey spectra shown in Fig. S3. The C1s region can be deconvoluted into four single peaks at about 284.3 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.4 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.9 eV, which are attributed to C = C, C-C, C-O/C-N, C-O-C, and O-C = O, respectively. The carboxylic acid peak occurred at 288.9 eV in Chl-CQDs due to the presence of the ester bond. And the existence of C = C peak further evidence the formation of CQDs coming from the origin groups of chlorophyll via ethanol induced solvothermal reaction. Meanwhile, the O1s peak of Chl-CQDs can be deconvoluted into three single peaks at about 532.3 eV, 533.8 eV and 535.1 eV, which are attributed to C = O, O-C = O and O-H, respectively, and evident the function of solvothermal reaction though the hydroxyl groups of ethanol[20]. Besides, the content of each element is shown in Fig. S4 based on the selected center and edge areas of Chl-CQDs. The center areas have the higher O/C atomic ratio, indicating higher amount of O-H, C-O, and C = O groups inner the Chl-CQDs structure and evidencing the coexistence of CQDs and chlorophyll. Therefore, it achieves the capsule structure and synergistic absorption via integrating the groups of CQDs and chlorophyll. Such synergistic absorption mode of Chlorophll/CQDs played a vital role in optimizing the photodetection performance of organic single crystals, as will be discussed later.

Meanwhile, room temperature photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed to investigate the interaction between the Chl-CQDs and DPA crystal. Fig. 2c shows the PL spectra of the DPA crystal, Chl-CQDs, and DPA crystal/Chl-CQDs heterostructure under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The spectrum peaks at 465 nm, 515 nm, and 680 nm in the heterostructure correspond well with the single components of the materials. In addition, a visible variation of PL intensity is observed in the heterojunction compared to the DPA crystal and Chl-CQDs film, indicating charge transfer between the two materials. Typical narrow optical band gap of OSCs exhibit a maximum absorption peak at 434 nm (Fig. 2d), while the Chl-CQDs display a wide and strong optical absorption range from 200 nm to 680 nm. Compared to the single-component material, the absorption spectrum of the DPA/Chl-CQDs heterostructure shows a significantly enhancement and slightly bathochromic shift, with peaks positioned at 377 to 380 nm, 400 to 402 nm, 430 to 432 nm, and 661 nm to 672 nm, respectively. These results confirm the successful construction of Chl-CQDs film onto the DPA crystals, forming a DPA crystal/Chl-CQDs heterostructure with an optimized synergistic absorption.

Fig. 3 a illustrates a circuit schematic and optical image of the heterotransistor. The HOMO level of DPA (−5.65 eV)[26] aligns well with the work function of the Au electrode and facilitates hole injection from the Au. The band gap and energy levels of Chl-CQDs are determined from the onset absorptions of the UV–vis spectra and ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). According to the initial absorption peak of UV–vis absorption spectrum, the optical band gap (Eg ) of Chl-CQDs semiconductors can be calculated as 1.85 eV by the linear extrapolation method based on the equation of
( α h v )
1 / m
= B
( hv -
E g
)
[27–31], where the α is the absorption coefficient, m is 1/2 (direct band gaps materials), B is constant, h is the Planck constant and v is the incident photon frequency (Fig. 2d).

The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) level is calculated to be 6.95 eV using −HOMO = hv − (Ecutoff − Eonset ), where hν = 21.21 eV is the photon energy of the He I light source and Ecutoff and Eonset are the high binding energy cutoffs and binding energy onset calculated via UPS (Fig. S5)[32,33]. The lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level is estimated to be −5.07 eV by combining the HOMO level and the energy band gap. Therefore, a type II band diagram (Fig. 3b) is confirmed based on the bandgap of DPA and capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs, as well as the estimated work function and valence band position. In these devices, band bending occurs at the surface of the junction and induces a built-in field, which facilitates the movement of electrons and holes in opposite directions and optimizes the photoelectric performance of the devices. Due to the higher work function of Chl-CQDs, holes are transferred to DPA while electrons are moved to Chl-CQDs when they come into contact. However, the charge transfer results in the formation of interfacial dipoles that impedes further hole injection into DPA single crystals. Nevertheless, with a large valence band offset of approximately 1.3 eV, the hole injection into DPA remains favorable under excitation in our devices. The representative p-type transfer curves of the Chl-CQDs/DPA heterotransistor are shown in Fig. 3c, where the device exhibits a reliably high hole mobility (μ) of 7.7 cm2 V−1 s−1 and a high on/off ratio of 3 × 108 under a bias of −40 V during multiple electrical measurements. The mobility in the saturation region is calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
μ =
2 L
W
C i
∂
I
SD
∂
V G
2
where L and W are the channel length and width, respectively, Ci is the oxide capacitance of 10 nF cm−2, and VSD (source-drain voltage) is the drain-to-source bias. The hybrid device also shows good contact between the Au electrodes and DPA single crystal, as demonstrated by the linear variation of the source-drain current (ISD ) with the bias voltage in the linear range of output curves (Fig. S6). In total, twenty heterotransistors are examined, and almost all of them exhibit varying degrees of improvement in carrier mobility compared to pristine DPA devices (Fig. 3d). These excellent results are mainly attributed to the compatible device preparation technology, high quality DPA crystals, and high charge separation efficiency of type II heterointerface with capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs structure.

Next, we examined the photodetection performance of Chl-CQDs/DPA-based heterotransistors to investigate the mechanism of charge transfer under light excitation, as shown in Fig. 4 a. To compare the photoelectric performance of heterotransistors with the pristine DPA single-crystal device, we tested their transfer curves under dark and illuminated conditions (white light) with a bias of −40 V, respectively. With an increase in the incident light power density from 27.6 to 450.0 μW cm−2, the threshold voltage (VT ) of both devices shifted positively and the ISD increased. The ISD of the hybrid phototransistor was significantly enhanced compared to that of the bare DPA device starting at 27.6 μW cm−2 illumination. Typical photo-induced transfer and output curves of both devices at different light power densities and bias conditions is plotted in Fig. 4b-e, which clearly illustrates that the heterotransistors have stronger photoresponse dynamics effects than the pristine DPA devices due to the significant increase in channel current. Based on the photocurrent measurements of the devices, parameters such as photosensitivity (P), photoresponsivity (R), and detectivity (D*) can be further evaluated using Eq. (2), (3)[34,35]:
(2)
P =
I light
-
I
dark
I
dark
(3)
R =
I light
-
I
dark
P
inc
(4)
D ∗ =
R
A
1 / 2
( 2 q
I
dark
)
1 / 2
where Idark is the drain current under darkness, Ilight is the photocurrent, Pinc is the incident laser power density, S is effective channel area of the device. q is the elementary electronic charge, and dark current density, respectively. The values of P, R and D* for the hybrid phototransistor, as a function of light intensity, are depicted in Fig. 4f-h. The highest P value reaches 3.2 × 107 and the maximum D* value reaches 6.4 × 1013 Jones (white light; 450 μW/cm2; VG = − 15 V). The maximum R value of over 8.2 × 103 A/W is achieved when the white light intensity equals 320 μW cm−2 with VG = − 40 V, implying a great potential in optoelectronic applications.

The time-dependent dynamic response of the hybrid device is shown in Fig. 4i, which demonstrates that the Chl-CQDs/DPA-based phototransistors have excellent reproducibility, stable, and reversible response to light illumination (white light; 260 μW cm−2; VSD = − 40 V, VG = − 10 V). More importantly, the device exhibits a fast response time (τrise ) of 35 ms and decay time (τdecay ) of 45 ms, as indicated in the inset of Fig. 4i, indicating that electron-hole pairs could be effectively generated and separated at the interface of the heterojunction. The photoconductance gain can be further estimated using Eq. (5) [36]:
(5)
G ph
=
τ life
τ
transit
where the value of τlife = 45 ms can be extracted from the ISD -time curve in Fig. 4i and carrier transit time can be estimated using τtransit = L 2/μVSD = 8 × 10-5 ms. Therefore, the device shows a high photogain greater than 2.8 × 105, which means that the device could recirculate multiple charge carriers upon irradiation by single incident photons. The excellent photoconductance gain may result from more efficient charge separation and recirculation due to the interfacial potential of the hybrid material. All the above values demonstrate the excellent optoelectronic properties of the novel Chl-CQDs/DPA phototransistors.

Fig. 5 a-c show the changes in key parameters before and after sensitization for the DPA single-crystal phototransistor under 450P conditions. Compared to the pristine DPA device, the hybrid phototransistor exhibits enhancements in P, R, and D* of over 6000 times, 5 times, and 200 times, respectively. Such enhanced photodetection in Chl-CQDs/DPA hybrid phototransistors are also demonstrated by a specific wavelengths of 365 nm ultraviolet light, where the hybrid device exhibits enhancements in P, D*, R of over 5000 times, 100 times, and 13 times than bare DPA phototransistor. These results forcefully show the high efficiency of the Chl-CQDs sensitized method for organic semiconductor single crystals.(Fig. S7), To deeply understand the reason of the enhancements, Fig. 6 exhibits the mechanisms of synergistic light absorption in Chl-CQDs and the charge separation efficiencies under different gate bias conditions in the Chl-CQDs/DPA heterotransistors. The light absorption of the ethanol extraction-based chlorophyll come from the tetrapyrrole macrocyclic rings shown in Fig. 6a-i, which are stabilized by a magnesium ion (Mg2+) in central position and form an extensive π-electron system[37]. Meanwhile, the tetrapyrrole macrocyclic rings are decorated with outlying substituents (e.g., phytol), which provides many carbonization locations for the formation of CQDs in the ethanol solvothermal process. Therefore, the formation of Chl-CQDs structure adds more π-π* transitions (come from the CQDs core structure) around the tetrapyrrole macrocyclic rings [24],which enhances the light absorption.

According to the FTIR spectrum, the carbonization and hydroxyl-induced-condensation enable the assembly of the CQDs surface and chlorophyll outlying substituents via C-C/C-O/C-N bonds. And these function bonds induce the new energy levels and further produce new electron transitions at the edge states of CQDs and build the high-density distribution of CQDs in the chlorophyll matrix[38], which enable the energy transfer among CQDs and chlorophylls in the Chl-CQDs structure. With the multiple π-electron systems, the capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs realized the synergistic absorption mechanism and provide an efficient way for fully enhancing the photoresponse of DPA single crystals (Fig. 6a-ii). Due to their work function difference, the interfacial dipole (φdipole ) at VG = 0 V stemmed from the initial electron injection from DPA to Chl-CQDs under light illumination. The formation of such dipole could impede further charge separation of photogenerated electrons and holes and therefore was prone to decrease the intrinsic photoresponse of Chl-CQDs/DPA hybrid devices[39]. At VG > 0, the strength of φdipole increases and the R of the p-type heterotransistors is suppressed, however, they still exhibit significantly enhanced P and D* compared to pristine DPA devices. The results are attributed to the high-efficiency charge separation and recirculation at the type II heterointerface based on a novel synergistic light absorption mode of Chl-CQDs and low-level dark channel current (Fig. 6b). At VG < 0, the φdipole is decreased and the charge recirculation channel become more efficient, which R increases with the rise of VG . However, due to the increase of dark current, the contribution of photo-induced carriers to channel current decreases gradually (gating effect increases gradually), resulting in the gradual decrease of P and D* [40]. It is noteworthy that the photoelectric parameters of hybrid transistor occur to slightly decrease than pristine device at a larger VG . This may be resulted from a higher dark current in such heterotransistors. These dramatic results demonstrate the valuable potential of exploring interfacial band alignment in type II heterostructures and gate modulation strategies for optimized OPTs.

In summary, we have demonstrated a novel sensitization method for organic crystalline materials by coating them with a novel capsule-shaped Chl-CQDs complex. The Chl-CQDs exhibit an interesting synergistic absorption behavior due to their abundant light absorption sites and efficient charge transfer ways based on multiple π-electron systems. Due to the high separation efficiency of such heterostructures and optimized device preparation technology, ultrasensitive light response speed (τrise = 35 ms and τdecay = 45 ms) of DPA single crystal is demonstrated through a type II Chl-CQDs/DPA interface, enabling efficient charge injection from Chl-CQDs to DPA upon illumination. Meanwhile, the constructed heterotransistors exhibit a high responsivity of 8.2 × 103 A/W (320 μW cm−2 with VG = − 40 V), a specific photosensitivity 3.2 × 107 of detectivity of 6.4 × 1013 Jones (white light; 450 μW cm−2; VG = − 40 V), all together with a high photogain of 2.8 × 105 under 27.6 μW cm−2. The key parameters of pristine DPA OPTs after sensitization are significantly enhanced by 6000 times (P), 250 times (D*), and 5 times (R), respectively. These encouraging findings demonstrate the great advantages of our proposed method in enhancing the photoresponse of OPTs and extending their potential applications in next-generation flexible optoelectronic circuits.

Yangyang Xie: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jinkai Wei: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yongxu Hu: Methodology, Investigation. Jianzhou Ren: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hongling Guo: Visualization, Validation, Software. Hao Lv: Validation, Supervision, Software. Chong Geng: Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Zhongwu Wang: Visualization, Validation. Shu Xu: Software, Project administration. Fang Wang: Validation, Supervision, Project administration. Lei Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kailiang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Liqiang Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

UPS of Chl-CQDs film; Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of DPA, Chl-CQDs film and DPA/Chl-CQDs heterojunction, Output curves of heterotransistors; Transfer curves under different UV illuminated conditions of DPA device before and after sensitization; The values of P, R and D* for the hybrid phototransistor under a series of UV light power intensities. Interfaces 10 (44) (2018) 38264-38271, doi: 10.1021/acsami.8b14408.
